choices.text: (1) Request memoization — dedupes identical fetch() calls within a single render pass; per-request, no manual invalidation needed. (2) Data cache — persists fetch results across requests/users/deployments until revalidation; invalidated by `cache: 'no-store'`, `next.revalidate`, or revalidateTag/Path. (3) Full route cache — caches rendered HTML for static routes; invalidated by revalidatePath or ISR. (4) Router cache — client-side cache of RSC payloads for visited routes; invalidated by router.refresh() or revalidation calls. Each layer has its own invalidation; debugging stale data starts by identifying which cache is serving the old version.

questions.explanation: Request memoization + data cache + full route cache + router cache.

questions.prompt: Server Action ran, mutation succeeded, but the page still shows old data after redirect. Which cache is stale?

choices.text: Data cache (and consequently full route cache if the page is static). The Server Action mutated the database, but the cached fetch results still hold the old data. Fix: call `revalidatePath(path)` or `revalidateTag(tag)` in the Server Action BEFORE the redirect. This invalidates the data cache and full route cache; the next render fetches fresh data. The router cache also gets cleared on next navigation via the same call. Forgetting revalidation after mutations is the most common Next.js bug.

questions.explanation: Data cache still has old. Call revalidatePath/revalidateTag in the action.
